name: a glycoside
annotation: A glycoside is any molecule in which a sugar group is bonded through its anomeric carbon to a non-acyl group via a glycosidic bond. Glycosides can be linked by a C-O bond (an O-glycoside), C-N bond (a glycosylamine), C-S bond (a thioglycoside) or a C-C bond (a C-glycoside). When the sugar is bound to a non-carbohydrate moiety, the sugar group is known as the glycone and the non-sugar group as the aglycone or genin part of the glycoside.

name: a pyrimidine
annotation: PYRIMIDINE-RING Pyrimidine is a heterocyclic aromatic compound similar to benzene, containing two nitrogen atoms at positions 1 and 3 of the ring. The name pyrimidines is also used for compounds derived from the basic pyrimidine compound, including Pyrimidine-Bases "three of the bases" found in nucleic acids, namely CYTOSINE, THYMINE, and URACIL. This class stands for all compounds that have a pyrimidine as part of their structure.

name: a pyrimidine ribonucleoside
annotation: Nucleosides are glycosylamines consisting of Bases bound to a CPD0-1110 ribose or 2-DEOXYRIBOSE sugar via a β-glycosidic linkage. The nucleosides are called Ribonucleosides ribonucleosides when the sugar is CPD0-1110 ribose, and Deoxy-Ribonucleosides deoxyribonucleosides when it is 2-DEOXYRIBOSE. Common Ribonucleosides ribonucleosides include CYTIDINE, URIDINE, ADENOSINE, GUANOSINE and INOSINE. Examples of Deoxy-Ribonucleosides deoxyribonucleosides include THYMIDINE and DEOXYADENOSINE. When nucleosides are phosphorylated on the sugar's primary alcohol group, the resulting phosphonucleosides are called Nucleotides nucleotides. Pyrimidine is a heterocyclic aromatic compound similar to benzene, containing two nitrogen atoms at positions 1 and 3 of the ring. The name pyrimidines is also used for compounds derived from the basic pyrimidine compound, including three of the nucleosides found in nucleic acids, namely CYTIDINE, THYMIDINE, and URIDINE.

name: α-NAD(P)H
annotation: α-NAD(P)H is a class of compounds including the instances α-NADH and α-NADPH. They should not be confused with the common electron carriers known simply as NAD(P)H, whose full name is β-NAD(P)H. The compounds differ in the stereo configuration of the bond between the ribose and the nicotinamide base, which points up in α-NAD(P)H and down in β-NAD(P)H.

name: a neolacto-series glycosphingolipid
annotation: Glycosphingolipids Glycosphingolipids are a family of complex lipids composed of Ceramides and mono- or oligosaccharide moieties that play an important role in various cellular functions including recognition, cell adhesion, proliferation and differentiation . The lipid portion is embedded in the outer plasma membrane leaflet and the sugar chains extended to the outer environment, acting as an antigen. Together with Glycoproteins glycoproteins and Glycosaminoglycans glycosaminoglycans, the glycosphingolipids contribute to the glycocalyx that covers eukaryotic cell surfaces. Glycosphingolipids are synthesized by the sequential addition of sugar residues to ceramide by glycosyltransferases that are specific to each glycosidic linkage. Glycoshphingolipids have been classified into the following 12 series based on their structure: Arthro-Series-GSLs arthro, Gala-Series-GSLs gala, Ganglio-Series-GSLs ganglio, Globo-Series-GSLs globo, Isoglobo-Series-GSLs isoglobo, Lacto-Series-GSLs lacto, Muco-Series-GSLs muco, Mollu-Series-GSLs mollu, Neogala-Series-GSLs neogala, Neolacto-Series-GSLs neolacto, Schisto-Series-GSLs schisto and Spirometo-Series-GSLs spirometo . Lactosyl-Ceramides Lactosylceramide is an important branching point, being a common precursor for the synthesis of the four major classes of glycosphingolipids: the Ganglio-Series-GSLs "ganglio series", the Lacto-Series-GSLs "lacto series", the Neolacto-Series-GSLs "neolacto series", and the Globo-Series-GSLs "globo series" . The core structure that defines the Neolacto-Series-GSLs "neolacto series" is Galβ(1,4)GlcNAcβ(1,3)Galβ(1,4)Glcβ-Cer In the diagram below, R1 describes the variable part of the sphingoid base, R2 describes the fatty acid, and R3 describes the glycan moiety.

name: a carboxamide
annotation: A carboxyamide is a compound with the general formula RC(=O)NR'R", where R, R', and R" represent organic groups or hydrogen atoms. It is often formed as a derivative of a carboxylic acid ,where the hydroxyl group is replaced by an amine group. The amide core group -C(=O)N- is called a peptide bond when it is part of the main chain of a protein, and an isopeptide bond when it occurs in a side chain, such as in the amino acids ASN and GLN. According to IUPAC, amides with NH2, -NHR, or -NRR' groups, where R and R' are groups other than hydrogen, should not be distinguished by means of the terms primary, secondary, and tertiary.

name: a purine
annotation: Purines are heterocyclic aromatic compounds that contain a PYRIMIDINE-RING ring fused to an IMIDAZOLE-RING ring. Two of the bases in nucleic acids, adenine and guanine, are purines. This class includes all molecules that contain purine as part of their structure.

name: a polyketide
annotation: Polyketides are secondary metabolites produced by some bacteria, fungi, plants, and animals. Polyketides are usually biosynthesized by Polyketide-Synthases "polyketide synthase" (PKS) enzymes through the decarboxylative condensation of extender units that are derived from malonyl-CoA, in a process similar to fatty acid synthesis. PKSs can be divided to two types: multifunctional polyproteins (type I) and discrete protein (type II). Type I PKSs are organized into modular complexes, each catalyzing individual chain elongation steps using an associated carrier protein. The minimal type I PKS module consists of 3 domains: a ketosynthase, a acyltransferase, and an acyl-carrier protein (ACP). The acyltransferase domain catalyzes the priming of the flanking ACP domain with the appropriate substrate. Type II PKS ACPs, on the other hand, possess catalytic self-acylation activity .

name: a [MAP kinase kinase]-(L-serine/L-threonine)
annotation: This generic class describes either an SER or THR within a mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase. In the case of SER, R = H, in the case of THR, R = CH3. Mitogen-Activated-Protein-Kinase "Mitogen-activated protein kinases" (MAP kinase or MAPK) are eukaryotic protein kinases that are specific to the amino acids serine, threonine, and tyrosine. They are found in all eukaryotes including unicellular eukaryotes, fungi, plants, and animals. MAP kinases are involved in directing cellular responses to a diverse array of stimuli, such as mitogens, osmotic stress, heat shock and proinflammatory cytokines. They regulate cell functions including proliferation, gene expression, differentiation, mitosis, cell survival, and apoptosis . MAP kinases are catalytically inactive in their base form. In order to be active, they need to be phosphorylated. Most of the MAP kinases belong to the "classical MAP kinases" family and need to be phosphorylated on two amino acids, a threonine and a tyrosine, which are found in a TxY motif at the activation loop. Phosphorylation of the tyrosine precedes phosphorylation of the threonine. Some MAP kinases are atypical and require only a single residue in their activation loops to be phosphorylated. MAP-kinase-MEK "Mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase" (also known as MAP2K, MEK, MAPKK) is the enzyme that phosphorylates MAP kinases. In humans there are seven genes encoding MAP2K enzymes. MAP2K enzyme also need to be activated by phosphorylation prior to becoming active. The activation is carried out by the serine/threonine protein kinase MAP kinase kinase kinase ( MAP3K or MEKK).

name: a pyrimidine base
annotation: Nucleobases are the parts of nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) that are involved in pairing of the two strands. The primary nucleobases are CYTOSINE, GUANINE, ADENINE (found in both DNA and RNA), THYMINE (found only in DNA) and URACIL (found only in RNA), abbreviated as C, G, A, T, and U, respectively. There are many additional nucleobases, usually derived from the primary ones. Based on their structure, nucleobases are divided into Purines purines and Pyrimidine-Bases pyrimidines. PYRIMIDINE-RING Pyrimidine is a heterocyclic aromatic compound similar to benzene, containing two nitrogen atoms at positions 1 and 3 of the ring. The name pyrimidines is also used for compounds derived from the basic pyrimidine compound, including three of the bases found in nucleic acids, namely CYTOSINE, THYMINE, and URACIL.

name: a sphingolipid
annotation: Sphingolipids are a class of lipids derived from the unsaturated aliphatic amino alcohol SPHINGOSINE. They are named for the mythological Sphinx because at the time (1884) their function was enigmatic . The sphingosine backbone (often referred to as the Sphingoids "sphingoid base") is amide-linked to an acyl group, such as a fatty acid, and is often also O-linked to a charged head group such as ethanolamine, serine, or choline. There are three main types of sphingolipids: Ceramides ceramides, Sphingomyelins sphingomyelins, and Glycosphingolipids glycosphingolipids. Ceramides are the most simple sphingolipids, composed of only the sphingoid base and a fatty acid. Sphingomyelins are composed of a ceramide bound to a charged head group, usually PHOSPHORYL-CHOLINE. Glycosphingolipids are composed of a ceramide with attached mono- or oligosaccharide moieties. Mammals usually produce PHOSPHORYL-CHOLINE-containing Sphingomyelins sphingomyelins, while plants and fungi usually produce glycosphingolipids with inositol as the head group.

name: a B vitamin
annotation: B vitamins are a class of eight water-soluble vitamins that play important roles in cell metabolism. The B vitamins were classified together because all of them serve as either enzyme cofactors, cosubstrates in important metabolic reactions, or as precursors for cofactors or cosubstrates. The different B vitamins are chemically distinct compounds that do not resemble one another. The dietary supplements known as "B complexes" contain all eight of these compounds. The eight members of the vitamin B class are: Vitamin B1: THIAMINE Vitamin B2: RIBOFLAVIN Vitamin B3: NIACINE, NIACINAMIDE, and NICOTINAMIDE_RIBOSE "nicotinamide riboside" Vitamin B5: PANTOTHENATE Vitamin B6: PYRIDOXINE, PYRIDOXAL, and PYRIDOXAMINE Vitamin B7: BIOTIN Vitamin B9: THF folate Vitamin B12: ADENOSYLCOBALAMIN (although supplements usually contain CPD-9037 or CPD-315, which are converted to the active form in vivo)

name: a glycoprotein
annotation: Glycoproteins are proteins that have been modified by the attachment of a glycan (carbohydrate) moiety. This process is known as "protein glycosylation". The most common forms of protein glycosylation include: N-linked glycosylation, where the glycan is attached to a nitrogen of asparagine or arginine side-chains. O-linked glycosylation, where the glycan is attached to the hydroxyl oxygen of serine, threonine, tyrosine, hydroxylysine, or hydroxyproline side-chains. Other, less common, forms of protein glycosylation include phosphoglycans, in which the glycan is linked through the phosphate of a phosphoserine, and C-linked glycans, where the glycan is attached to a carbon on a tryptophan side-chain. A special form of protein glycosylation is glypiation, in which a protein is attached to a lipid anchor via a glycan chain.

name: a cobamide lower ligand
annotation: Cobamides "A cobamide" is a corrinoid molecule based on the structure of CPD-20843, a cobalt-corrinoid hexaamide that is formed from COBINAMIDE by the attachment of an α-D-ribofuranose 3-phosphate residue at the 2 position of the aminopropanol chain. Cobamides serve as cofactors for a number of important enzymes. A key structural feature of cobinamides is a corrin ring with a cobalt ion in its center. Four of the six coordination sites of the cobalt ion are provided by the corrin ring, while the other two are provided by Cobamide-Upper-Ligands upper (β) and Cobamide-Lower-Ligands lower (α) ligands, which are located above and below the ring plane, respectively. The best known cobamide, Cobalamins cobalamin, contains DIMETHYLBENZIMIDAZOLE as the lower ligand and either a methyl group or 5'-deoxyadenosyl group as the upper ligand. At least 16 different cobamides with structural variability in the lower ligand have been described. The 16 bases reported so far are BENZIMIDAZOLE, CPD-18320, CPD-18321, CPD-18497, CPD-18495, DIMETHYLBENZIMIDAZOLE, ADENINE, CPD-18323, GUANINE, HYPOXANTHINE, PHENOL, CPD-108, CPD-5723, CPD-20865, CPD-20864, and CPD-20885 . Different cobamides may not be functionally equivalent as cofactors for cobamide-dependent enzymes .

Glycoshphingolipids have been classified into the following 12 series based on their structure: Arthro-Series-GSLs arthro, Gala-Series-GSLs gala, Ganglio-Series-GSLs ganglio, Globo-Series-GSLs globo, Isoglobo-Series-GSLs isoglobo, Lacto-Series-GSLs lacto, Muco-Series-GSLs muco, Mollu-Series-GSLs mollu, Neogala-Series-GSLs neogala, Neolacto-Series-GSLs neolacto, Schisto-Series-GSLs schisto and Spirometo-Series-GSLs spirometo . annotation: A glycosyl compound is a carbohydrate derivative arising formally from the elimination of water from a glycosidic hydroxy group and the attachment of the exposed carbon atom to an oxygen, carbon, nitrogen or sulfur atom of a second entity from which an H atom has been removed.

name: a deoxy sugar
annotation: This compound class stands for any sugar having a hydroxy group replaced.

name: an aliphatic sulfonate
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified aliphatic sulfonate compounds.

name: a ring
annotation: EcoCyc compound Rings

name: a purine in DNA
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified DNA purines.

name: an aldotetraose
annotation: EcoCyc compound Aldotetraoses

name: a polymer segment enzyme substrate
annotation: This class contains reaction substrates that stand for a segment or section of an extended polymer, like a nucleic acid. An enzyme might typically recognize a limited motif on the polymer, but the detailed sequence of the full length polymer will not matter in such reactions.

name: a furanoside
annotation: A furanoside is a glycoside where the sugar moiety is in a furanose form.

name: a nonspecific small molecule
annotation: EcoCyc compound Compounds-Holder-Class

name: a nucleoside phosphate group
annotation: EcoCyc compound Nucleoside-Phosphate-Groups

name: a carboxylate derivative
annotation: EcoCyc compound Carboxylate-Derivatives

name: a long-chain fatty aldehyde
annotation: Fatty-Aldehydes "Fatty aldehydes" are aldehydes that are formed by the reduction of the carboxylic acid group of Fatty-Acids fatty acids. Many enzymes that act on fatty acids, as well as the corresponding Fatty-Acyl-CoAs "fatty acyl-CoAs", Fatty-Alcohols alcohols, and Fatty-Aldehydes aldehydes, have evolved to recognize substrates with a limited range of chain lengths. To classify these enzymes, it is helpful to divide the substrates into smaller groups based on their chain length. The following subgroups have been defined: Short-Chain-Aldehydes "Short-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Short-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 3-5 carbons in the longest chain". Medium-Chain-Aldehydes "Medium-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Medium-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 6-12 carbons in the longest chain". Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Long-Chain-Fatty-Acids "fatty acids that have 13-22 carbons in the longest chain". Very-Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Very-long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Very-long-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 23-27 carbons in the longest chain". Ultra-Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Ultra-long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Ultra-long-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have more than 27 carbons in the longest chain".

name: an acyl group
annotation: an acyl group is a functional group derived by the removal of one or more hydroxyl groups from an oxoacid. This broad definition includes functional groups derived from inorganic oxoacids, however, in organic chemistry the acyl group is almost always derived from a carboxylic acid.

name: a nucleobase-containing molecular entity
annotation: This class stands for any compound whose structure contains a nucleobase.

name: a C-methylated amino acid
annotation: EcoCyc compound C-Methylated-Amino-Acids

name: Unknown-Inducers
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified inducers.

name: a C-nitro compound
annotation: This class contains compounds that have a nitro group (-NO2) attached to a carbon atom.

name: a cyclic dinucleotide
annotation: EcoCyc compound Cyclic-Dinucleotides

name: an organic acid
annotation: An organic acid is any organic molecular entity that is acidic and contains carbon in covalent linkage.

name: a phosphoric ester
annotation: EcoCyc compound Phosphoric-Esters

name: a [receptor protein] (L-serine/L-threonine) phosphate
annotation: This generic class describes either a phosphorylated SER or phosphorylated THR within a receptor protein. In the case of SER, R = H, in the case of THR, R = CH3.
family_key: sm:name:a receptor protein l serine l threonine phosphate

name: a galactosaminoglycan
annotation: Glycosaminoglycans Glycosaminoglycans consist of hexosamine and either hexuronic acid or galactose units that are arranged in alternating unbranched sequence, and carry sulfate substituents in various positions. The common glycosaminoglycans include Galactosaminoglycans galactosaminoglycans (Chondroitin-sulfates and Dermatan-Sulfate) and Glucosaminoglycans glucosaminoglycans (Heparan-Sulfate, HEPARIN, KERATAN-6-SULFATE and Hyaluronan).

name: a sulfur-containing carboxylate
aliases: S-containing carboxylic acid|S-containing carboxylic acids|sulfur-containing carboxylic acids
annotation: This compound class stands for any carboxylic acid that has a sulfur substituent.

name: a [protein]-L-glutamate
annotation: No structure is available for this compound class, since it includes both Protein-alpha-L-Glutamates and Protein-gamma-glutamates.

name: a [protein]-L-β-isoaspartate
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified protein L-β-aspartates.

name: an aminopyrimidine
annotation: Aminopyrimidines are members of the class of pyrimidines in which the pyrimidine is substituted by at least one amino group, as well as derivatives.

name: α-NAD(P)+
annotation: Alpha-NAD-P is a class of compounds including the instances CPD-16008 and CPD-16007. They should not be confused with the common electron carriers known simply as NAD-P-OR-NOP, whose full name is β-NAD(P)+. The compounds differ in the stereo configuration of the bond between the ribose and the nicotinamide base, which points up in α-NAD(P)+ and down in β-NAD(P)+.

name: an organic hydroxy compound
annotation: This class includes any organic compound that has at least one hydroxy group attached to a carbon atom.

name: a secondary amino compound
annotation: Secondary amino compounds are compounds formally derived from ammonia by replacing two hydrogen atoms by organyl groups.

name: a double-stranded oligonucleotide
aliases: an oligonucleotide(n-1)|an oligonucleotide(n+1)|an oligonucleotide(n)|a ds oligonucleotide
annotation: An oligonucleotide is a short nucleic acid polymer with 50 or fewer bases. Oligonucleotides can be either single- or double-stranded.

name: a phosphorus oxoacid and derivatives
annotation: EcoCyc compound Phosphorus-oxoacids-And-Derivatives

name: an N-glycosyl compound
annotation: N-glycosyl compounds are glycosyl compounds arising formally from the elimination of water from a glycosidic hydroxy group and an H atom bound to a nitrogen atom, thus creating a C-N bond.

name: a ketoaldehyde
annotation: A ketoaldehyde is a compound that has both an aldehydic group and ketonic group.

name: a non-proteinogenic α-amino acid
annotation: This compound class stands for α amino acids other than the 20 standard protein building amino acids. An α amino acid is defined as any molecule that contains both amine and carboxyl functional groups, attached to the same carbon atom.

name: an aliphatic α,ω-diamine
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified α,ω-diamines.

name: an organonitrogen compound
annotation: This class stands for any heteroorganic entity containing at least one carbon-nitrogen bond.

name: a phosphoric acid derivative
annotation: EcoCyc compound Phosphoric-Acid-Derivatives

name: an O-short-chain-acyl-L-carnitine
annotation: This class contains CARNITINE molecules esterified at the C3 hydroxyl to short-chain fatty acyl groups .
family_key: sm:name:an o short chain acyl l carnitine

name: an oxacycle
annotation: An oxacycle is an organic heterocyclic compound containing at least one ring oxygen atom.

name: an aliphatic amide
annotation: EcoCyc compound Aliphatic-Amides

name: a generic polynucleotide substrate
annotation: This class stands for general polynucleotide substrates, to be used in general reactions such as polynucleotide modification. It is not the same as the super class Polynucleotides, which includes all polynucleotides in the database.

name: an O-(long-chain-acyl)-L-carnitine
annotation: This class contains CARNITINE molecules esterified at the C3 hydroxyl to long chain fatty acyl groups .
family_key: sm:name:an o long chain acyl l carnitine

name: an N-hydroxy-arylamine
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified N-hydroxy-arylamines.

name: a nucleoside
annotation: Nucleosides are glycosylamines consisting of Bases bound to a CPD0-1110 ribose or 2-DEOXYRIBOSE sugar via a β-glycosidic linkage. The nucleosides are called Ribonucleosides ribonucleosides when the sugar is CPD0-1110 ribose, and Deoxy-Ribonucleosides deoxyribonucleosides when it is 2-DEOXYRIBOSE. Common Ribonucleosides ribonucleosides include CYTIDINE, URIDINE, ADENOSINE, GUANOSINE and INOSINE. Examples of Deoxy-Ribonucleosides deoxyribonucleosides include THYMIDINE and DEOXYADENOSINE. When nucleosides are phosphorylated on the sugar's primary alcohol group, the resulting phosphonucleosides are called Nucleotides nucleotides.

name: a C-nitroso compound
annotation: This class contains compounds that have a nitroso group (R-N=O) where the nitrogen is attached to a carbon atom.

name: a dinucleotide (nucleic acid)
annotation: This generic class describes molecules composed of two nucleotides that participate in nucleic acids. For a class that describes dinucletoide-type electron carriers, such as NAD and NADP, please see Dinucleotide-Electron-Carriers.

name: a D-aldose
annotation: An aldose is a monosaccharide containing one aldehyde group per molecule and having a chemical formula of the form CnH2n-1On where n>=3. R in this structure drawing stands for CnH2nOn where n>=2.

name: a 2-oxo acid
aliases: a keto acid|a α keto acid|2-oxo acid
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified 2-oxo acids.

name: an organic heterocyclic compound
annotation: EcoCyc compound Organic-heterocyclic-compound

name: a [protein with EGF-like domain]-(L-serine/L-threonine)
annotation: This generic class describes either an SER or THR within a protein that contains an EGF-like domain. In the case of SER, R = H, in the case of THR, R = CH3.
family_key: sm:name:a protein with egf like domain l serine l threonine

name: an ADP-D-ribosyl acceptor
annotation: EcoCyc compound ADP-D-Ribosyl-Acceptors

name: a porphyrin
annotation: The porphyrins are a class of heterocyclic macrocycles derived from CPD-26422, a molecule that consists of four Pyrroles pyrrole rings joined together by four methine (=CH-) groups to form a larger macrocycle ring. Many porphyrins occur in nature, and fulfil important biochemical roles. Porphyrins can bind metals to form complexes. The metal ion, usually with a charge of 2+ or 3+, resides in the central cavity formed by the loss of two protons. Many metals are found within different derivatives, including Fe, Mg, Ni, Co and Zn. The name porphyrin comes from a Greek word for purple, since most porphyrins are red to purple in color. More reduced porphyrins, such as Corphins corphins, are yellowish.

name: a lysophosphatidate
annotation: A lysophosphatidate is a generic term that covers a GLYCEROL-3P with a single acyl group attached, either at the 1 or 2 position.

name: an organic cyclic compound
annotation: EcoCyc compound Organic-Cyclic-Compounds

name: an entity with an SNFG structure
annotation: This class holds compounds that may possess an SNFG structure. Symbol Nomenclature for Glycans (SNFG) is a system for symbolic representation of glycans. The system assigns unique graphical symbols for monosaccharides, and provides a much simpler representation for complex glycans, depicting both the monosaccharide building blocks and their linkages within the glycan structure. The system is widely accepted in the literature and adopted by many major journals. Updated information about the SNFG system is available here.
family_key: sm:name:an entity with an snfg structure
display_name: an entity with an SNFG structure

name: a furan
annotation: This class stands for any compound that contains at least one CPD-15491 ring. Furan is a five-membered aromatic ring with four carbon atoms and one oxygen, containing two double bonds.

name: a glycosyl ester
annotation: EcoCyc compound Glycosyl-Esters

name: a CpG site
annotation: The CpG sites or CG sites are regions of DNA where a cytosine nucleotide occurs 5' to a guanine nucleotide. "CpG" is shorthand for "-C-phosphate-G-", that is, cytosine and guanine separated by a single phosphate group. The "CpG" notation is used to distinguish this linear sequence from the CG base-pairing of cytosine and guanine. Cytosines in CpG dinucleotides are often methylated to form 5-methylcytosine. In mammals, 70% to 80% of CpG cytosines are methylated .

name: a straight chain 2,3,4-saturated fatty acid
annotation: This class holds 2,3,4-saturated fatty acids that are unbranched.
family_key: sm:name:a straight chain 2 3 4 saturated fatty acid

name: a nucleotide conjugate
annotation: EcoCyc compound Nucleotide-Conjugates

name: an α,β-unsaturated carbonyl compound
annotation: α,β-Unsaturated carbonyl compounds are an important class of carbonyl compounds in which the carbonyl group is conjugated with an alkene. They have the general structure (O=CR)-Cα=Cβ-R. Unlike simple carbonyls, α,β-unsaturated carbonyl compounds are often attacked by nucleophiles at the β carbon. Many of these compounds are toxic, mutagenic and carcinogenic.

name: a glycoconjugate
annotation: Glycoconjugate is the general term used for carbohydrates that are covalently linked with other chemical species such as proteins, peptides, lipids and saccharides. Examples for glycoconjugates include Glycoconjugates glycoproteins, glycopeptides, Peptidoglycans peptidoglycans, Glycolipids glycolipids, Glycosides glycosides and Lipopolysaccharides lipopolysaccharides. Glycoconjugates are often involved in cell-cell interactions, cell-cell recognition, cell-matrix interactions, and detoxification processes.

name: a 1,4-β-D-glucan
annotation: This compound class stands for 1,4-β-D-glucosyl polymers.

name: a nucleoside diphosphocholine
annotation: EcoCyc compound Nucleoside-Diphosphocholines

name: a [protein]-amino acid
aliases: protein amino acid|a peptidyl amino acid|an amino acid within a protein
annotation: This class represents amino acids that are incorporated within a protein. (It does not represent the free form of the 20 amino acids that are naturally incorporated within proteins).

name: a ketohexose
annotation: This class includes any hexose that contains a single ketone group.

name: an adenylated D-amino acid
annotation: This class contains D-amino acids that have been activated by AMP.

name: a dinucleotide electron carrier
annotation: This class is specific for the dinucletoide-type electron carriers, such as NAD and NADP. For a class that represent two nucleotides that participate in nucleic acids, please see Dinucleotide-Nucleic-Acid.

name: a sugar phosphate
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified sugar phosphates.

name: an amine
annotation: Amines are derivatives of AMMONIA, with one or more of the hydrogen atoms replaced by organic substituents such as alkyl and aryl groups. When only one of the three hydrogen atoms in ammonia is replaced by an alkyl group, the compound is called Primary-Amines. When two, three, or four of the hydtogen atoms are replaced, the compound is called Secondary-Amines, Tertiary-Amines, or Quaternary-Amines, respectively.

name: a nucleic acid component
annotation: This class includes several classes of compounds that form components of nucleic acids, and serves as a way of grouping those classes together. It does not strictly speaking define a structurally defined set of compounds.

name: an organic amino compound
annotation: This class stands for compounds formally derived from ammonia by replacing one, two or three hydrogen atoms by organyl groups.

name: a positively-charged polar amino acid
annotation: This class stands for all of the positively-charged polar amino acids. It contains both α and non-α amino acids.
family_key: sm:name:a positively charged polar amino acid

name: an adenylate
annotation: This class holds compounds that have been activated by conjugation to AMP "adenosine 5'-monophosphoate" (AMP).

name: a ceramide
annotation: A ceramide is a lipid molecule that is composed of Sphingoids and Fatty-Acids. Ceramides are found in high concentrations within the cell membrane and are one of the component lipids that make up Sphingomyelins sphingomyelin, one of the major lipids in the lipid bilayer.

name: an organic heterobicyclic compound
annotation: EcoCyc compound Organic-heterobicyclic-compounds

name: a β-D-galactoside
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified β-D-galactosides.

name: an mRNA
annotation: This class stands for general mRNA substrates, to be used in general reactions such as mRNA modification. It is not the same as the super class mRNAs mRNAs, which includes all mRNAs in the database.

name: a bacterial endotoxin
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified bacterial endotoxins. These are components from the outer membrane and cell wall of bacteria, which are toxic to humans and can cause sepsis. The Gram negative bacteria produce lipopolysaccharides, whereas the Gram positive bacteria produce the less active lipoteichoic acids and muramyl peptides.

name: an aromatic compound
annotation: An aromatic compound contains one or more aromatic rings (an aromatic ring is a planar ring in which every atom must have an occupied p orbital, which overlaps with p orbitals on either side). The pi orbitals of the ring's atoms form a conjugated system and are in resonance, giving increased stability compared to saturated compounds having a similar structure. As a result aromatic rings are very stable and do not break apart easily. The least complex aromatic hydrocarbon is benzene. The name stems from a time when compounds were characterized by their smell rather than their chemical structure. Similar structures in which at least one of the ring's carbon atoms is replaced by one of the heteroatoms oxygen, nitrogen, or sulfur, are called heteroarenes.

name: an ether lipid
annotation: This class describes lipids similar in structure to a glycerolipids but in which one or more of the carbon atoms on glycerol is bonded to an alkyl chain via an ether linkage, as opposed to the usual ester linkage to an acyl chain.

name: a cyclic amide
annotation: EcoCyc compound Cyclic-Amides

name: a peptidoglycan cleavage product
annotation: This class includes compounds that are produced by cleavage of peptidoglycan polymers.

name: an O-medium-chain-acyl-L-carnitine
annotation: This class contains CARNITINE molecules esterified at the C3 hydroxyl to medium-chain fatty acyl groups .
family_key: sm:name:an o medium chain acyl l carnitine

name: non-specific ion/solute
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified ions or solutes in transport reactions. Once a given transporter is better characterized, a more specific compound class should be substituted instead.

name: a phthioceranoyl-CoA
annotation: This class stands for the acyl-CoA form of Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids". In heptamethylated phthioceranic acids R1 = H. In octamethylated phthioceranic acids R1=CH3.

name: hydrophilic solute or ion < 600 Da
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified ions or hydrophilic solutes in transport reactions. They have a molecular mass of smaller than 600Da. Once a given transporter is better characterized, a more specific compound class should be substituted instead.

name: a ferric hydroxamate complex
aliases: an iron (III) hydroxamate complex|an iron (III) hydroxamate
annotation: This pseudo compound class stands for generic and unspecified complexes between a ferric iron ion and a hydroxamate compound.

name: a glycan group
annotation: Information about the proper nomenclature of GylcoCT entries was retrieved from MonosaccharideDB, which is developed by Thomas Lütteke as part of the EUROCarbDB and GLYCOSCIENCES.de projects.

name: a non-α amino acid
annotation: This compound class contains non-α amino acids, that is, any molecule that contains an amine and a carboxyl functional groups, attached to different carbon atoms.

name: a pyrimidine nucleoside in DNA
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified DNA pyrimidiness.

name: a methylated amino acid
annotation: EcoCyc compound Methylated-Amino-Acids

name: a sugar derivative
annotation: The term "sugar" is usually applied to Monosaccharides monosaccharides and Disaccharides disaccharides, Oligosaccharides oligosaccharides and Polysaccharides polysaccharides as well as substances derived from monosaccharides by reduction of the carbonyl group (Sugar-alcohols alditols), by oxidation of one or more terminal groups to Carboxylates "carboxylic acids", or by replacement of one or more hydroxy group(s) by a hydrogen atom, an amino group, a sulfhydryl group or similar heteroatomic groups. It also includes oligo- and polysaccharides consisting of such modified monosaccharides, as well as derivatives of these compounds. Even though the carbohydrate definition includes derivatives, we list substances generated by further modifications (e.g., acetylation, phosphorylation) under this class as "a carbohydrate derivative" for clarification purposes.

name: Superatoms
annotation: This class consists of molecular fragments that can be used by the Pathway Tools superatom subsystem. Instances of this class should not be used as substrates of reactions.

name: a hydroxylated fatty acid derivative
annotation: EcoCyc compound Hydroxy-fatty-acid-derivs

name: an imino group
annotation: EcoCyc compound IMINO-GROUP
